author: @westonstevens3239
comment:
I found a lot of companies that do ask coding questions don't even ask stuff on LeetCode, they will ask stuff they came up with internally that may not even be found anywhere on the web. If you become a LeetCode expert you are relegating yourself to being successfully hired by just a small subset of the companies out there, most do not even ask coding questions. They focus on your knowledge of technologies, system design, and your experience. So if you only focus on just LeetCode or even coding questions in general that will make you almost unhireable to everyone else. 

And mastering coding questions to the point that you have a coin flip chance to come up with the right solution for any given coding question asked of you is just about the hardest thing there is to do, there are literally millions of them out there and to have a chance at getting any of these questions right you had to have reviewed it at some point in your life. And by reviewed I mean practically memorized.

And while learning coding questions is nonetheless productive so is learning and keeping up with technologies, knowing the right answer to give for any situational question you are presented with, knowing how to code a complex program in a given framework (e.g. a full stack web application), mastering SQL and NoSQL, mastering the system design and so forth are hard enough by themselves and cannot be ignored either. What I like to do is just review a coding question here and there rather than going all-in because I want to have a chance to be hired by the majority of companies out there not just a handful.

Also lastly its important to be well rounded not just for the sake of being able to pass most interviews but also so that you can more ably do the job once you get past the interview and you don't find yourself in an impossible situation.

author: @Nick-f4w2n
comment:
I should get started on this. I'm doing codingame problems when I don't have anything to do at work (my job is unrelated to programming) and it's by far the most difficult thing I've ever done. Problems that sound easy are incredibly difficult. Try doing a breadth first search with nobody telling you how to do it. You try some stuff with lists, it doesn't work. Do some arrays of arrays, it doesn't work. You create a complicated dictionary, it doesn't work. Even when I was on the right track, my algo got stuck in an infinite loop of searching A->B->C->A. I've actually made custom software related to my job, and none of it was anywhere near as difficult as solving a 6 degrees of kevin bacon problem.

In all seriousness, I think retired people should learn programming. Your mind stays sharp if you're always trying to solve problems you've never faced before.

author: @awaresiel1738
comment: Either you dont have data or you are being disingenuous. While LeetCode grinding builds familiarity, raw intelligence—especially fluid reasoning—is a major differentiator in top-tier interviews like those at Google. Cognitive science consistently shows that individuals with higher IQs are significantly better at identifying patterns, combining techniques, and avoiding dead ends. An average-IQ candidate may know the same patterns but is statistically more likely to take incorrect first steps, pursue misleading paths longer, and take more time to realize and correct mistakes. In contrast, a high-IQ candidate is more likely to make accurate initial guesses, adapt faster to pattern twists (e.g., combining two pointers with binary search), and complete problems under time constraints. Practice narrows the gap, but raw intelligence still shifts the odds.

author: @thetranquilears
comment:
Commenting even for my own future reference:

I believe the problem arises from rushing. You get stuck in a comfy job, getting a okayish pay but you know you are under paid and deserve more. Suddenly you are browsing linkedin and a friend of yours posts they are starting a new job at a Tier 1 firm and getting good money now. You get frustrated that while you were planning and making strategies, someone outworked you and now is in a better position. 

The frustration is what drives you to pursue the uncharted and now you want to grind leetcode within a month and crack a good company. But to your surprise, since you became comfy, now you lack skills, because your daily job wasn’t challenging enough. Hence you are not able to solve easy problems which makes you frustrated. Heck you even apply to jobs and get OA links but you see that you are straight away bombing those tests. 

Its mid of 2025 and one has to up their game to stay relevant. These days its easy to ask AI to summarise all of leetcode problems and you can memorise it. But trust me even if you get the job this way, in no way you will survive. 

Thus, my point is, leetcode is often associated with a frustrated engineer. That should not be the case. This video explains it very well. If you don’t have the habit of comp programming, you will struggle and its fine. Take baby steps to learn the fundamentals and write them down, whatever you learn. Revise Revise and Repeat. If you have good grasping, in a few months you will be in a position to solve mediums quite well. Don’t be frustrated where you are and by what others are doing. Nobody shows the failures and nobody shows the grind. Keep your head down, be physically and mentally healthy, and study.

author: @workhard5971
comment:
00:00 Learning DSA and core algorithms is important for high-paying job opportunities, even though Leetcode is academically difficult and requires perseverance. Collapse
Grinding Leetcode is not necessary for most full stack jobs, but the speaker will provide simple points on how to approach it effectively.
Learning DSA and core algorithms is still useful, even if it may seem useless, because grinding Leetcode can lead to high-paying job opportunities, as it did for the speaker at Google.
Leetcode is the most academically difficult thing the speaker has ever done, even more so than college, and it is important to understand that it is harder than all other classes and requires perseverance to become familiar with it.
🧠
02:27 Leetcode is about systems and processes, not intelligence, and the speaker learned by using online resources and providing clear explanations for solutions. Collapse
Leetcode can be challenging, but it's not about intelligence, it's about systems and processes, and the speaker learned by going to the solutions tab when stuck on difficult problems.
The speaker discusses using online resources to find solutions and explanations for Leetcode problems, highlighting the importance of finding clear and understandable explanations.
In the past, there was little explanation for Leetcode problems, but now the speaker makes videos to provide intuition and understanding of solutions.
🧠
05:02 Practice and repetition are essential for learning Leetcode, as it requires a step-by-step process similar to learning math, and many struggle because they attempt problems beyond their skill level without mastering the basics first. Collapse
Learning Leetcode is not easy because the methodology of learning it is different from learning math or physics, and it requires a step-by-step process similar to how children learn math.
Practice and repetition are essential for learning and retaining difficult concepts, as simply understanding something once does not guarantee the ability to do it again later.
Many people struggle with Leetcode because they attempt problems beyond their skill level without mastering the basics first.
🧠
07:39 Master the basics and core algorithms, practice repeatedly, and don't be discouraged by the time it takes to understand and solve Leetcode problems. Collapse
Master the basics before moving on to more advanced problems, and don't be discouraged if it takes time to understand and solve them.
Repetition and practice are essential for learning Leetcode, and the speaker realized that their previous approach was not sufficient.
Learn and practice core algorithms like binary search and DFS before solving Leetcode problems to improve your skills.
Learn and practice a short algorithm repeatedly to be able to implement it quickly and easily when solving Leetcode problems.
🔍
11:18 Implementing algorithms multiple times gives a big advantage in solving Leetcode problems and reinforces understanding. Collapse
Understanding and implementing binary search is easy and efficient, requiring repetition to master.
Implementing algorithms multiple times gives you a big advantage in solving Leetcode problems, making medium problems easier and reinforcing your understanding.
🧠
13:02 Understanding the why behind math and algorithms is crucial for excelling at Leetcode and competitive programming. Collapse
To excel at Leetcode and competitive programming, one must treat it like learning the ABCs, even if it's not always fun, and the next level of improvement involves smart repetition and practice.
Understanding the why behind math and algorithms is crucial for solving problems effectively.
🧠
15:08 Spend time understanding concepts, practice with examples and creative edge cases instead of memorizing. Collapse
Spend time thinking about and understanding the concepts rather than trying to memorize them.
Practice by walking through examples, use pen and paper when stuck, and think of creative examples and edge cases to reinforce understanding.
📝
17:17 Repetition, structure, and handwritten notes from childhood are useful for learning, and having too much freedom as an adult can lead to procrastination.

author: @a_red_turtle
comment:
@NeetCodeIO But that is even a grandiose take. 90% of leetcode problems boil down to applying binary search, BFS/DFS, Hash tables, and sorting (maybe 1 or 2 more techniques). 

Even the 'hard' problem you posted is just a tiny adaptation of binary sort. 

Perhaps you had good teachers or easy classes, but even ignoring that physics/math/etc... are orders of magnitude more broad, the mathematics involved in all of them is often intangible and unrelated to common experience. Leetcode, on the other hand, is essentially almost always a book-keeping problem and something akin to very elementary combinatorics.

Anyway, apologies for being so antagonistic, I like your videos. I just can't understand this take.

author: @zachary_burrows
comment:
If you have a CS minor or at least a few programming courses LeetCode full time is probably 100hrs of GOOD studying (which is very hard to do more than a few hours a day)  away from being ready to interview in big tech. Another 100hours for industry hire / mid level. Maybe tack another 50hrs on there for the current market and a sprinkle of luck.

Sticking to something long term that's so challenging take a lot of intention to stay positive and focus on the outcome. Also trying to timebox a sprint and put yourself on a schedule. Otherwise doing 2-4 problems a week will help you maintain and grow slowly

author: @Nick-f4w2n
comment:
IMO, you also need to be able to explain to someone what the actual problem is. Suppose we have a social network and we each circles of friends, or neighbor nodes we could call them. How would you calculate the shortest path between us with the fewest jumps? Like I know Suzie, Suzie knows Steve, Steve knows you, so you and I are 3 jumps apart. I was banging my head against the wall trying to figure out how this could possibly work. Forget actual programming. If I handed you a felt pen and asked you to write down in very vague terms what to do, would you be able to give an answer?  
The best/worst problems on leetcode are ones where you have no idea where to start.

author: @chakreshsingh
comment:
Everything you said, is a 100% correct. But the reason I don’t see Leetcode same as Maths or Physics is because unlike the physical sciences or maths, I do not find the same intellectual curiosity in me when solving these dry coding problems. I don’t find matrix multiplication in < O(n^3) as intellectually stimulating. I don’t find, reversing a linked list in-place, as interesting a partial differential equation or a kinematic problem from I E Irodov’s book.

I have cracked these interviews and have solved over 400 problems on Leetcode. Still, it doesn’t attract me the way I am attracted to Mathematics. 

We are tired fighting a battle, we don’t want to be in.

author: @ImranBrown
comment:
Subtle thing to add here. 
1. Master your language and then master how to use it in a Leetcode environment. There are many functions and things you would never write in production code but if speed is required, you better now how to initialize a 2D matrix with -1's, or maybe a set. How to join arrays of arrays. 
Neetcode is correct anytime you lose time on the "basics", it will be distracting and will take away time from solving the edge case or deriving the unique trick in that problem. 
2. This is job security, especially in this market. Until the industry does not use this as some type of IQ test, keep yourself up to date. Try to do 2 a week AFTER you get a job. That comes out to about 104 questions a year. This is one of the biggest things I see people not do, so they end up restarting and feeling hopeless again because they stop practicing.

author: @GenericInternetter
comment:
16:22 examples! yes!

one of my high school teachers (science? maths?) taught me to always test what you're doing with seven samples:
the min and max "correct" values, the "middle" (or expected) value, values that are halfway between the middle and min/max, and finally values that are a little past the min and a little past the max (outside the "correct" range).

this advice has helped me immensely over the years with many many things, even in daily life.

for example: if i'm lerping an input value between two target values 50 and 125, i'd estimate (guessing *at a glance* without calculating) that the values would be 45, 50, 70, 90, 110, 125, 130
to ensure everything is working correctly, i'd input 0.0, 0.5, 1.0 etc and see if the outputs are as expected.
if i see a 200, a 0, or a -50 show up, i instantly know something went very very wrong with the input or how it is processed.

author: @andreab04
comment:
answering questions really goes down to following these steps:

1. Walk through test cases to clarify the problem and what it is doing.
2. Ask clarifying questions, especially about edge cases (a unique/unclear way to handle problem)
3. Pattern Cognition: Big-O Notation (time/space complexity), DSA and identify what to use
    - Check constraints 
4. Implementation: practice the patterns
5. tackle ways to debug (is it a syntax or implementation error?)

author: @VantaCanadaBlack
comment:
The key is realizing LeetCode is stupid and the questions are stupidly written and not being able to "figure it our" doesn't mean anything, the way to do LeetCode is try it for 5 minutes then if nothing is happening look at the answer immediately understand it (Almost every question has multiple YT explanations now) and write the solution in a way that makes sense to you code wise, a lot of the "solutions" are written stupidly in cryptic and incomprehensible ways because people equate complexity or shortness/one liners with being smart but what you actually want is clear readable code. 

Then move on to the next question rinse repeat, every couple of weeks circle back through the questions and try to solve them again if nothing makes sense look at your answer immediately and don't beat yourself up about it, rinse repeat. Always remember LeetCode is stupid and practically useless in your actual work but you might need it to pass an interview but it is not a measure of skill or ability to be a software developer.

I think the same lesson is mentioned here. If you want to be able to solve medium questions faster, you need to be practiced at the easy questions that you can get the assignment done faster in a 45 minute interview where you only have 20-30 min to code.

author: @igorku7369
comment:
I should have started on this path years ago, but yes, I was lazy. 
Just as the author of this video says, not knowing the basics will eventually catch up with you. That is exactly what happened to me now. 
I am a self-proclaimed programmer, with no formal CS education, not to mention the DSA training. I used to get new jobs in the past 10 years of my web-developer experience literally within a week. Sometimes I worked for just a few months, but some jobs I landed lasted for 2-3 years. Nobody cared about DSA during the interviews, as there were plenty of jobs and a lot of employers were satisfied with getting employees who were just like me - the self-proclaimed programmers, if not people off the street. And that, of course, taking into consideration that indeed - a lot of the DSA knowledge is not even applicable in the real world web programming. I guess I did my job well enough to be that lucky.
These days, however, I cannot even get to the tech interview phase of recruitment process. It is certainly explainable - approx 500 000 pretty good programmers have been laid off in the past couple of years (I think at least a third of them are the very good ones from FAANG who obviously know what the Leetcode is). Hence there are at least 1000 programmers per vacancy. Can you imagine now my chances of getting a job even if I get to the tech interview phase?
So, speaking of the author - thank you for the reminder that not everything is lost, but that it takes quite an effort to get good at anything, given the simple reinforcement techniques learned in the elementary school (such as repetition) are followed.

author: @blablaqwertyful
comment:
Why don't people revisit solved problems? Because the don't want to. Why? It's not rewarding enough. Most of these problems are too abstract and disconnected from real-world situations. Take 'Android Unlock Patterns' as an example. Sure, the answer is 42, but then what? What was the actual purpose? Why would I ever care about knowing that?

Even when they look useful the solution often feels completely irrelevant. The famous 'coin change' problem, for instance, asks for the number of combinations. Is that really what you need from a vending machine? 'Hey, customer, I can give you change in 231048 ways!' Very practical, right?

Then there's the 'Campus Bikes II' problem, which asks for the 'minimum possible sum of Manhattan distances between each worker and their assigned bike.' It’s utterly meaningless: 'Hi, delivery guys, I have no idea who gets which bike, but the minimal distance is 42. Now go complete your deliveries.' Extremely practical. By the way, interesting things happen with time and space complexity when you try to provide a meaningful answer.

The issue isn’t that LeetCode problems are hard; it’s that you have to trick your brain into feeling rewarded for solving a problem you have no interest in. For the solution to really stick, it needs to be grounded in something real, something your brain can recognize as a worthwhile achievement. Are there problems like these? Not too many, I'd say.

author: @demoncorejunior
comment: the real problem is that employers treat these academic problems as relevant to assessing someone's ability to write business code for money. as you said, you had to learn to do these problems in order to get a high-paying job, not because it's particularly useful to you otherwise. in 25 years in the industry i have not needed to supply the time complexity of a some code i was forced to write in 30-45 minutes, not even once. in fact, it's rare for me to need to care about the efficiency of the code i'm writing, because it's generally not low-level/high-throughput software. my laptop can run it locally, with everything in memory. and this is all at FAANG-scale companies. the real job skills are about being able to read thousands of other peoples' code, add some of your own, and figure out how to get it to work with all the other software in the company. it's almost never a green field project, so many constraints based on the existing environment have resolved potential trade-offs. good luck on the leetcode grind though, it's an unfortunate requirement to get in the door most places.

What got me back into this was actually using DFS in a real world design of building unique paths through a visual drag/drop graph. I'd really pay for something that did this with real projects. Here's some that ended up being real for me:
1. Build a graphical UI that calculates paths through the nodes via the edges and executes a predefined action on each node.
2. Compare the contents of two files. If a line is present in the second file but not the first, treat it as an insert operation. If it is not present in the second but is in the first, treat it as a delete operation. If it is present in both but any values after the first in the csv line are different, treat it as a modification.

author: @hous2700
comment:
​@sondosahmed4870 it's important but not to the extent that you focus only on it. 

The benefit of leetcode is not solving problems, its what it actually does to your brain. 
You go to the gym to get stronger muscles, similar to leetcode. You get stronger at thinking and problem-solving skills doing leetcode problems, but if you're goal is to become a software developer, there is a lot more to learn then simply solving problems.

Examples: 
1. leetcode problems are solved in basically one method but that doesn't work in a project because in projects, you have to write a single method that has a single purpose (ex: a method simply to calculate 1+1 and if you want to print it, you create another method to print it).
2. Building this many single purpose methods/functions require managmenets skills. How can all these methods/functions connect with eachother and work togather to achieve the desired result.
3. Namings. In leetcode problem, it really doesn't matter how you name your method, but in projects, names are very important because you and other developers can understand them and what their purpose is instead of spending a lot of time everytime you go back to see them.
4. Documentation
5. Requirements and System Analysis.
6. System Architecture

And I can go on and on...etc.

And actually, even projects is basically, you trying to solve problems and one project can have many problems you need to solve but the process is very different than leetcode. 
Why? Because problems in your project affect the whole system. So you have to make sure that when solving a problem, it doesn't affect other solutions otherwise you will end up redoing all your project because it turned out a mess (I've been here before haha).

I haven't started leetcode yet but for me, the best way for a software developer is to combine solving leetcode problems and doing actually projects. You can solve like 2 or 3 problems everyday and the rest of your time, you focus on core software engineering skills.

Good luck buddy.

author: @rustyshackleford8022
comment:
I dont think this really lines up. You don't (or at least i hope you dont) learn math by grinding problems in a vacuum. You should be taught principles and then practice them to generate your own intuitive understanding and rote memorizations (ex learn addition and then taught multiplication is cumulative addition then practice to memorize the tables). 

Your advice is to work backwards and grind a priori data into memory and (hopefully) derive the principles later. If you have an understanding of the necessary axioms it should be pretty easy to just learn the principles of an algorithm and then all you need is 'rote it' (and learn anything posteriori).  On the other hand in my AP hs math class most ppl didnt understand how equations fundamentally work so I don't expect that to be so plausible for majority of people. The key issue of course is if you are a 'grinder' and you run into something beyond your grind you will be stumped.

I guess it's good advice for dumb people but it's also like: why are they trying to be programmers and would you really want to hire them?

author: @amitarora0623
comment:
Notes : - Mistake - People are doing superficially e.g Solving a Medium Level Binary Search problem when they cant even implement a native binary search algo. They look up and then forget after a week. The biggest thing missing here is **Repetition**. Remember the repetition that you used to do while in college ? 
    - Solution - Your first target should be to get very very good at solving the basic algos like 
        - DFS 
        - BFS 
        - Binary Search -> Less than 10 lines of code .
        - Sliding Window etc. 
    - Aim to write code for these problems in less than 5 mins. Generally - First time takes 30 mins, 2nd time it takes 10 mins, 3rd time 9 mins and 5th time - 5 mins and 30 times would get you to less than 30 mins :).  So, thats how you can do for each. 
    - Working on basic algos makes sure that You save a lot of time which can be used for actually APPLYING this algo to the problem at hand. 
    - Once this works, try to ask "**Why this works**". Spend some time figuring this out on your own. Take a pen and paper and try to walk through the Why on your own.

author: @mj6400
comment:
At 16:22 it's not more that people are lazy, it's that people don't have the cognizance or information to create a real-world scenario based on something new to them. If someone is learning a new verbal language, the only way they would be able to connect to a saying or word is to correlate it to something familiar. Leetcode provides examples that are only relatable to programmers, or akin to something from a college course. For anyone new to Leetcode, or programming, the examples don't correlate to anything they've seen nor do they connect (all the time) to real-world objects. Essentially, it's asking someone to place their imagination on something that seems static and concrete, which could discourage someone from seeing it.

A solution to what you're saying, however, can be solved by AI. Allow CGPT, Claude, etc. to read the problem and give real-world examples and scenarios of when it will be used. AI can be a Teachers Assistant for Leetcode, but, it should be used only as that and not as a quick answer submission machine.

author: @magiclover9346
comment: Software engineer and musician. Repetition is good, boring but good. A session musician can recognise a song structure and read the music in one go because they've spent hours at home practising scales and intervals. Training their ears to recognise patterns. Coding has many similarities. Instead we are training our brains to recognise problems and connect solutions to those problems. Algorithms, Datastructures and Patterns are our scale and interval study equivalent. Do them enough and itll become second nature. I was reviewing a team members code the other day and immediately recognised he was using DFS to solve a problem.

author: @valentinrafael9201
comment: I also play chess and do chess puzzles for fun. There is a method there called "woodpecker method". You solve a chess problem, and then you redo it 10 times before moving to the next ( refreshing the table so you don't see the moves ). It works especially well when the puzzle is longer and has more moves, because when you redo it, you really have to understand it before attempting again.

author: @education-xn8bk
comment:
Advice on how to tackle leetcode .

leetcode - system , methodologies , process 3:04 , 
Steps : Do bunch of repetition like - when you used to practices maths multiplication . many times .
first target : practices core algo - like binary search , dfs , sliding  - implement 5 times 10 times over a period of month
second target : why somethings works ?  why algo works ? - spend a little bit time think , curious about it . 
walkl thorough an example using paper and pen . using creative solution , using edge cases .

important thing that was done when you were kid - handwritten notes .  explainig your  thought process , repetition , boring repetition like math enough times . Structures like one hour for english , 1 hour for maths works .

I was lying on my bed and got up to leave a comment here, my laptop was on my desk... You literally transformed my developer life, in a perfect way. 

I started my career as a web developer 3 years ago. After three months, I took a new React project, since then, I have been working as a Frontend developer. 

Last year, I moved abroad and started to seek opportunities abroad. 
In my country, I already had my job and had network, I didn't worry that much to get a position, more like how much I could get or what company I worked for.
 
One year ago, I started to apply for jobs and I got rejected by all companies I applied - Language may've been the biggest problem though as I started to talk in English since last year.

I didn't want to miss opportunities if I got. I tried to solve leetcode several times but I didn't find any interest in it.
Actually, I was the person thinking code test is not practical to apply real projects and maybe a waste of time. 

As a frontend developer, I may've focused on frontend technologies, builders, cloud platforms and stuff like that or better to study DS, and someday, your video came out on my youtube list. 

Thanks to the algorithm. I actually saw some of your videos early last year but I couldn't understand at all due to my Enlgish proficiency, and last Winter, when your video came out on mylist, I clicked it and I was able to understand a bit and watched your videos. 

It was an absolutely amazing moment. Your explanation was SO easy, after watching your videos, every problem felt easy to be solved. 

At around that time, there was a company I was supposed to join but the company asked me to withdraw the contract a couple of weeks before starting to work, they said budget problems but I don't think so. After that, I had to go back to my country, but I really didn't want to GIVE UP. 

On the first day, I came back to my country, I started to solve Top 150 problems with your videos. As I remember, I couldn't solve most of problems without your help. And at some point, I started to solve some problems by myself and I noticed changes inside me. 

I started to think first, and come up with some solutions. Like you said, sliding windows, dfs, bfs, some other stuff, while solving problems, I got used to those concepts.
I don't need to think about how to implement those stuff. I could just use it as my tool. It's not about memorizing solutions. 
It's about how to define a problem, how to see a problem and how to apply knowledge I have to solve the problem. It's all about thinking. 
I also noticed that I apply the same way to solve leetcode problems, when I create UI components in React, write logics and approach errors. 

I'm still not goot at leetcode and still need to improve A LOT, I know but I felt the positive changes and now I know if I do it, I could do better as time goes, as I put more effort on it. 

Thank you very much. I'm still looking for a job now but it does not matter, I just do code, I like it and I like those changes. 

Once again, thank you very much for sharing your experience, knowledge and thought with us. Good night 👍

author: @EmilyHopeGrove
comment:
it's just practice. why isn't everyone doing it. 

OK so - this is not, NOT an excuse, this is an explanation. 

While children have a watchful teacher and parent (hopefully) to guide them and prod them to practice and do homework, and they spend 5 days out of the week, 10 months out of the school year, over 12+ years, they are doing the learning. Because it's their job. They are, in theory, being housed, clothed, and fed (hopefully) by their parent or guardian. Obviously not everyone is in this category, but a good chunk are.  

Now, fast forward to the adults trying to learn. they do not likely have a direct teacher, perhaps they do, but let's be honest, a lot of us come to Youtube etc because we need to learn something but we do not have a teacher. Right, ok. So - do we spend 8 hours a day, 5 days a week, 10 months a year to learn? No, not likely. Most of us have other adult responsibilities. Ie, paying the rent, buying the groceries, making those groceries into the fuel we need to stay alive, and hey, probably holding some kind of job to afford these things. So, our time becomes very limited. Our mental and emotional power becomes limited. The few hours a day I have to sit down and make myself do the thing, practice and try to figure it all out, is very limited. When I don't jump out a window because of the insanity of how the tech world is gatekeeping their well-enough paying jobs to keep you alive and maybe retire someday, with this type of hoops to jump through, it's a win of a day. Sp, unless you have someone out there taking care of your life needs, and you do actually have at least 8 hours a day to dedicate to your learning, you are not very likely to be in a situation where practice can be done regularly, and intentionally, beyond a precious few hours a day, here and there. It's not a great system our world provides to any of us who are attempting to break into the industry. 

Anyways. Not bitter. Just ...aware.


Anyways, practice makes better, keep going guys. your next interview or job offer is coming, if you keep going.

author: @sandman.38
comment:
To me it’s not about the questions, it’s about the uncertainty of the interview process and how people view you as a candidate relative to ‘n’ other candidates. Leetcode questions are meant to be solved on paper and then typed up using the same mental logic you used to solve the problem. We solve problems every day, it’s quite simple, and all you need to know to succeed at LC style questions is pattern recognition for each question type + understanding time / space complexity + DS + search algos and that’s about it. System design is literally a walk in the park, same thing, mental logic. But it’s just the uncertainty of the interview process that makes this shit hard to cope with tbh. You’re always second guessing yourself because of how uncertain things are, especially today lol. 

Like I need a job to survive, and these jackasses are trying to find a math olympiad just to refactor their legacy ObjC code like f off, just hire me haha.

The reason people bash on leetcode or doing/learning DSA is because we depend mostly on libraries people have built. Many atime, depending on the problem/work one is depending, you will still need to develop an algorithm to solve a problem, and the efficient the algorithm is, the better it is for the software you are working on. Improving my DSA knowledge, trying leetcode problems and studying leetcode solutions have been impactful on me. The few engineers i have met that i consider to be amazing are good at DSA (and applying it). They are usually able to build automation tools and make things much more efficient.

In summary, DSA and practicing how to apply them are very important for building softwares and tools. Many enginers/evelopers these days hate putting in enough to be better or build better solutions

author: @smartkorean1
comment:
To a lesser extent, this advice reminds me of my Theory of Computation class. 

When you’re asked to build a NFA or PDA that recognizes a certain language, the first step is knowing all the basic patterns like how a machine keeps track of even / odd characters, how it detects a substring, how it counts a certain amount of characters. 

Only after knowing these basic patterns are you able to build more complex machines. And with anyone of these problems, it’s best to write some strings that are in the language and some that are not, along with some edge cases that could break the machine 

Hopefully someone else can relate and hopefully apply it to their leetcode grind, good luck :)

author: @joedorben3504
comment: Because none of these methods with chess training actually work (as far as what people want). The reality is, your innate talent is gonna cap anybody who wants to get good. Everyone plateaus around some natural limit, for 99.99% of people who get into chess, that limit is gonna be around 1600-2000, however when someone reaches that level they're gonna want to become a master (2200) which is a far more significant leap than what came before. And if/when anyone becomes a master (again, already extremely few players out of the general population of players), 95% or more won't be able to get much stronger than that. Even just a jump as simple as CM to FM is massively hard for many, and going to IM is huge too. When you reach IM, it feels like GM is within reach, but again it's a massively wide gap which most do not ever manage to cross. But to be honest, even many people who train chess won't even make 2000, which isn't that high anyway. Most who did not play from childhood are going to cap out around 1400-1800, which again is far below where anyone who gets into chess would want (although in online rating they can hit much higher due to the volatility there). Bottomline is, no chess training method is going to be widely or generally used for other fields, because in most cases, it won't even work for chess the way its users want it to.

author: @t74devkw
comment: Algorithms and data structures, as required in technical interviews and as present in LeetCode, are the subject matter of master’s programs in computer science. Everything beyond a simple binary search is, indeed, not an easy topic at all; it is a very academic topic. What makes it even harder is the fact that the average software engineer, most often than not, has never even opened calculus (e.g., Stewart or Spivak) and algorithms (e.g., Cormen or Skiena) textbooks; it’s only natural that all those algorithms feel alien to someone seeing algorithms for the first time.

uh bro its not masters, if you want an internship within your first 2 years you need system design, leetcode mediums, and real work projects that arent basic projects. you're supposed to do calculus>discrete math>computer arch so you know how binary works on a motherboard before python. if you dont youre not an engineer at all youre a struggling self proclaimed coder

author: @itsjustramblings
comment: The coding assessment don't test you algo knowledge but primarily if you can practice and recall from memory when you need to apply algorithms. I come from a non-cs background but even i knew lot of ds and algos but i never faced any hard complicated puzzle that require more than 1 algorithm to solve until about mid-2010's. Once an interviewer asked me a fib series with memoization that allowed me to get past the first round. These days interviews specifically ask hard type problems to weed as many candidates as possible, it is not you but the market. And interviewers are also very impatient and hard to handle these days, if you make a tiny mistake your whole work experience will questioned. It is pathetic.

author: @asagiai4965
comment:
No, as for me leetcode is not hard. (At least for a lot of questions). There are only few occasions that is considered hard.
I think people are confusing hard to intricate (or with intricacies).
Technically, most people here who solve problems, have done a lot of leetcodes in their life.

If I have advices to be good at it,  learn DS, Algo, Solve some problem (not leetcode) here and there 
(the problem doesn't need to be hard, but it should give you an introspective look at yourself) 
Then after is the time you do some leetcode. To check your knowledge or something.

Also, the comparison to physics and calculus is weird, though. 
I mean, there is no leetcode physics. If there's leetcode physics, college students are fckd. 

But if you want to enjoy leetcoding sure go for it.
